section.title: You Have Searched Me and Known Me
section.sectionRationale: One of the most intimate psalms in the Psalter, this meditation on God's total knowledge, inescapable presence, and personal forming of every life in the womb stands among the closing Davidic psalms of Book V, its final prayer, search me and know my heart, modeling the self-examination that flows rightly from being fully known and fully loved.
section.thesis: The LORD who knows every thought before it is spoken, who is present in the highest heaven and the darkest depth, who knit the psalmist together in the womb and wrote every day of his life in a book before one of them came to be, is a God whose total knowledge is cause not for dread but for worship, ending in the humble invitation, search me, O God, and lead me in the way everlasting.
section.frameNote: Four stanzas of six verses each mark the psalm's movement: God's total knowledge of me, verses 1-6; God's inescapable presence with me, verses 7-12; God's forming of me in the womb, verses 13-18; and the psalmist's allegiance against evil and invitation to God's searchlight, verses 19-24.

units.label: You have searched me and known me

verses.text: O LORD, You have {{chaqar|searched}} me and known me.
You know when I sit and when I rise; You understand my thoughts from afar.
You search out my path and my lying down; You are aware of all my ways.
Even before a word is on my tongue, You know all about it, O LORD.
You hem me in behind and before; You have laid Your hand upon me.
Such knowledge is too wonderful for me, too lofty for me to attain.

units.label: Where can I flee from Your presence

verses.text: Where can I go to escape Your Spirit? Where can I flee from Your presence?
If I ascend to the heavens, You are there; if I make my bed in Sheol, You are there.
If I rise on the wings of the dawn, if I settle by the farthest sea,
even there Your hand will guide me; Your right hand will hold me fast.
If I say, "Surely the darkness will hide me, and the light become night around me,"
even the darkness is not dark to You, but the night shines like the day, for darkness is as light to You.

units.label: You knit me together in my mother's womb

verses.text: For You formed my inmost being; You {{raqam|knit}} me together in my mother's womb.
I praise You, for I am fearfully and wonderfully made. Marvelous are Your works, and I know this very well.
My frame was not hidden from You when I was made in secret, when I was woven together in the depths of the earth.
Your eyes saw my unformed body; all my days were written in Your book and ordained for me before one of them came to be.
How precious to me are Your thoughts, O God, how vast is their sum!
If I were to count them, they would outnumber the grains of sand; and when I awake, I am still with You.

units.label: Search me, O God, and know my heart
units.note: allegiance against evil, and God's searchlight invited

verses.text: O God, that You would slay the wicked, away from me, you bloodthirsty men,
who speak of You deceitfully; Your enemies take Your name in vain.
Do I not hate those who hate You, O LORD, and detest those who rise against You?
I hate them with perfect hatred; I count them as my enemies.
Search me, O God, and know my heart; test me and know my concerns.
See if there is any offensive way in me; lead me in the {{olam|way everlasting}}.

chaqar.gloss: To search out, to probe thoroughly, used of mining into rock. God's searching is not a suspicious investigation but a thorough, intimate knowledge of every corner of a life, which David finds too wonderful to fully grasp, verse 6.

raqam.gloss: To weave or embroider, used here of God knitting the psalmist together in the womb. The image is of careful, skilled handiwork, God as artisan forming every unborn child with the same personal attention He gives every other part of creation.

olam.gloss: Everlasting, ancient, without end. The way everlasting is both the enduring path of righteousness and, ultimately, the path that leads into eternal life with God, the goal of David's closing prayer to be searched, tested, and led.

basic.context: David marvels that the LORD already knows every thought, word, and movement of his life before it happens, is present in the highest heaven and the deepest darkness alike, and personally formed him in the womb, writing every day of his life in a book before any of them began.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm is a meditation, not a philosophical treatise; David describes what it feels like to be fully known by an all-knowing, ever-present, personally involved Creator, and moves from that description to a prescriptive response: hatred of evil aligned with God's own heart, and humble invitation for God to search and lead him.
basic.originalLanguages: God has chaqar, searched, David thoroughly, the same word used for mining deep into rock, yet this searching feels too wonderful, too lofty to attain, verse 6. In the womb God raqam, knit, wove David together with the care of a skilled artisan. The psalm closes asking to be led in the way olam, everlasting, both a lifelong path of righteousness and the path into eternal life.
basic.typology: David's confession that God's eyes saw his unformed body and ordained every day before one of them came to be, verses 15-16, anticipates the New Testament's teaching that believers were chosen and known by God before the foundation of the world, Ephesians 1:4, and that Christ Himself was known before He was formed in Mary's womb, Luke 1:31-35.
basic.christ: The searching, ever-present God of this psalm is revealed in Christ, who knew what was in man without needing to be told, John 2:24-25, and who invites the same searching self-examination David seeks, testing hearts even now, Revelation 2:23, while offering grace to those He finds and forgives.
basic.god: God is shown as fully omniscient, knowing every thought before it forms, fully present everywhere, from the heights of heaven to the depths of Sheol, and fully involved as Creator, personally forming every human life in the womb and writing its days before they begin.
basic.crossReferences: Jeremiah 1:5 echoes God's knowledge of a person before formation in the womb. Hebrews 4:13 restates that nothing in creation is hidden from God's sight. Ephesians 2:10 grounds human dignity and purpose in being God's handiwork. Revelation 2:23 shows Christ Himself searching hearts and minds.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's vision of a God who knows every thought, is present everywhere, and personally forms every life in the womb runs through the whole Bible's insistence on human dignity from conception onward, and finds its fulfillment in Christ, who both searches hearts perfectly and, having been formed in a womb Himself, leads all who invite His searchlight into the way everlasting.

context.literary: A meditation in four balanced stanzas of six verses each, moving from God's knowledge, to God's presence, to God's forming work in the womb, to the psalmist's allegiance against evil and closing invitation for God's own searching.
context.historical: Headed for the choirmaster, a psalm of David, suggesting both temple use and a deeply personal, mature reflection, likely composed later in David's life after seasons of both triumph and moral failure had taught him how fully known he already was by God.
context.canonical: Placed among the closing Davidic psalms of Book V, this psalm's meditation on being fully known by God gives theological depth to the whole Psalter's confidence that the LORD who searches hearts is also the LORD who saves and leads them.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To marvel at God's total knowledge, inescapable presence, and personal forming of the psalmist's life from conception, and to let that wonder produce both allegiance against evil and humble openness to God's ongoing examination and guidance.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's own experience of being fully known, present with, and formed by God; it prescribes the abiding pattern of responding to that knowledge with worship, moral seriousness about evil, and an invitation for God to search and lead the heart.

debates.question: How should verses 19-22, do I not hate those who hate You, be reconciled with Jesus' command to love enemies?

views.label: As zeal for God's honor rather than personal vendetta, a category distinct from private retaliation and compatible with loving one's personal enemies
views.case: David's hatred is directed at those who hate God and speak of Him deceitfully, an allegiance to God's cause rather than personal revenge, and Scripture elsewhere commands love even while opposing what is evil, Romans 12:9.

views.label: As an Old Testament expression of covenant loyalty later refined, though not overturned, by Christ's fuller revelation of love for enemies
views.case: The New Testament deepens how that opposition to evil is expressed, calling believers to pray for and love even those who oppose God, while still hating what is evil, so the emotional intensity of the psalm is taken up into a fuller ethic rather than simply repeated.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the psalm expresses zealous allegiance to God rather than personal spite, and both find that allegiance completed, not cancelled, in Christ's call to love enemies while still hating evil itself.

study.originalLanguages: God has chaqar, searched, David thoroughly, using the same word for mining into rock, yet this searching feels too wonderful to attain, verse 6. In the womb God raqam, knit, wove David together as a skilled artisan. The closing prayer asks to be led in the way olam, everlasting, both a lifelong path of righteousness and the path into eternal life with God.

typology.type: God's eyes seeing the unformed body and ordaining every day before it began, verses 15-16
typology.fulfillment: Believers chosen and known by God before the foundation of the world, Ephesians 1:4, and Christ Himself known and formed in the womb, Luke 1:31-35.

typology.type: The closing prayer, search me, O God, verse 23
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who searches hearts and minds even now, Revelation 2:23, offering grace to those He finds and forgives.

study.christ: The all-knowing, ever-present God of this psalm is revealed in Christ, who knew what was in every person without needing to be told, John 2:24-25, and who now searches hearts and minds, Revelation 2:23, inviting the same self-examination David welcomes while offering grace to all who are found wanting.
study.god: God is fully omniscient, knowing every thought before it is formed, fully present everywhere from the highest heaven to the deepest darkness, and fully involved as Creator, personally forming every human life in the womb and ordaining its days before they begin.

crossReferences.note: Echoes God's intimate knowledge of a person before he was formed in the womb, paralleling verses 13-16.
Restates that nothing in all creation is hidden from God's sight, the New Testament's own summary of verses 1-12.
Grounds human purpose and dignity in being God's own handiwork, echoing the psalm's forming imagery.
Shows the risen Christ Himself searching hearts and minds, fulfilling the psalm's closing invitation.

study.oneStory: This psalm's vision of a God who knows every thought, is present everywhere, and personally forms every life in the womb undergirds the Bible's insistence on human dignity from conception onward, fulfilled in Christ, who searches hearts perfectly and, having been formed in a womb Himself, leads all who invite His searchlight into the way everlasting.

sermon.title: Doing the Right Thing Never Ruins Your Life: A Belief That Prevents Abortion
sermon.why: Piper works directly from Psalm 139:13-16 to show that God's forming and knowing of a person in the womb grounds the sanctity of unborn life, applying the psalm's central confession pastorally and practically, exactly the value of human life this psalm is meant to teach.

quiz.q: What does David marvel at in verses 1-6?

options.text: That the LORD already knows his every thought, word, and movement before they happen, knowledge too wonderful for him to attain.
options.note: Verses 1-6 describe total, intimate divine knowledge that leaves David in awe rather than dread.

options.text: That God cannot know his private thoughts.
options.note: The passage says the opposite, that God knows a word before it is even on David's tongue, verse 4.

options.text: That other people know him better than God does.
options.note: The entire stanza contrasts God's superior, total knowledge with any human knowledge of David.

options.text: That God's knowledge of him will end when he dies.
options.note: The psalm assumes ongoing knowledge and presence, extending even into Sheol, verse 8, not a limit at death.

quiz.q: According to verses 7-12, where can a person go to escape God's presence?

options.text: Nowhere, God is present in the highest heavens, the depths of Sheol, the farthest sea, and even in darkness.
options.note: The stanza systematically lists every extreme, height, depth, distance, darkness, to show God's presence is inescapable.

options.text: In the darkness, since darkness hides a person from God.
options.note: Verse 12 explicitly says darkness is not dark to God, but shines like the day to Him.

options.text: Only in foreign lands outside Israel's borders.
options.note: The imagery is cosmic, heaven, Sheol, sea, not limited to national borders.

options.text: In the depths of the sea alone, but nowhere else.
options.note: The sea is only one of several places named; God's presence extends everywhere the psalm mentions.

quiz.q: What does David confess about his forming in the womb, verses 13-16?

options.text: That God personally knit him together, saw his unformed body, and wrote all his days in His book before any of them began.
options.note: Verses 13-16 describe God's intimate, purposeful involvement in forming David before birth.

options.text: That his formation in the womb was purely a random biological accident with no divine involvement.
options.note: The passage explicitly credits God with forming, knitting, and ordaining David's days from before birth.

options.text: That God only became interested in him after he was born.
options.note: The text says God's eyes saw him while still unformed, before birth, not only afterward.

options.text: That his days were left undetermined until he chose them himself.
options.note: Verse 16 says his days were written and ordained before one of them came to be, not left to be self-determined.

quiz.q: What is the psalm's closing request in verses 23-24?

options.text: Search me, O God, and know my heart; test me and see if there is any offensive way in me, and lead me in the way everlasting.
options.note: This closing prayer is the natural response of someone already fully known, inviting further examination and guidance.

options.text: A request that God stop searching him and leave him alone.
options.note: The prayer is the opposite, an invitation for God to search and examine even more closely.

options.text: A demand that God punish David's enemies immediately.
options.note: That request appears earlier, verses 19-22; the closing prayer turns inward toward self-examination and guidance.

options.text: A request for wealth and long life above all else.
options.note: The closing request is for moral examination and guidance in righteousness, not material blessing.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's teaching on being formed in the womb connect to the New Testament?

options.text: It anticipates believers being chosen and known by God before the foundation of the world, and Christ Himself being formed and known in Mary's womb.
options.note: Ephesians 1:4 and Luke 1:31-35 both extend this psalm's confession of God's purposeful forming of life before birth.

options.text: It has no connection to any New Testament teaching.
options.note: Passages like Ephesians 1:4 and Jeremiah 1:5 directly echo this psalm's theology of being known before formation.

options.text: It only applies to David personally and to no one else.
options.note: Scripture applies this same truth broadly, to Jeremiah, to believers generally, and to Christ Himself.

options.text: It contradicts Jeremiah's account of being known before birth.
options.note: Jeremiah 1:5 closely parallels rather than contradicts this psalm's claim of being known before formation.

questions: David finds it comforting, not threatening, that God knows every thought before it forms. Does God's total knowledge of you feel like comfort or dread right now, and why?
There is nowhere David can go, height, depth, or darkness, to escape God's presence. Where do you find yourself trying to hide from God, and what would it mean to trust His presence there instead?
David praises God for personally forming him in the womb, before birth. How does this shape the way you think about the value of every unborn life, including your own?
David's zeal against evil in verses 19-22 flows from love for God's honor, not personal grudge. Where might your own anger need to be examined to see whether it serves God's glory or your own pride?
The psalm ends with an invitation, search me, O God, and lead me in the way everlasting. What would it look like this week to actually invite God's searchlight into a specific area of your life?

translationNotes.note: Inmost being renders a Hebrew word literally meaning kidneys, an idiom for the seat of one's deepest inner self, similar to how English uses heart.
How precious to me are Your thoughts carries a footnote possibility, how amazing are Your thoughts concerning me, both readings expressing wonder at the vastness and personal focus of God's thoughts.

Your enemies take Your name in vain is textually difficult; a footnote notes the Hebrew could also be read as Your enemies take Your cities in vain, though the sense of irreverent misuse of God's name fits the surrounding verses best.

BSB em dashes in verses 11 and 19 were converted to commas per house style; footnote markers stripped from the running text and preserved in translationNotes. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming John Piper's 'Doing the Right Thing Never Ruins Your Life' at desiringgod.org, Scripture Psalm 139:13-16.
